event: Hunter-gatherers settle the Swiss plateau as glaciers retreat at the end of the last Ice Age.
Neolithic farming communities establish lakeside pile-dwelling settlements around Swiss lakes.
The Celtic Helvetii dominate the Swiss plateau during the Iron Age La Tène culture.
Julius Caesar defeats the migrating Helvetii at the Battle of Bibracte, drawing the region into the Roman orbit.
Roman forces under Augustus complete the conquest of the Alpine regions, incorporating modern Switzerland into the Empire.
Alemannic tribes break through the Roman frontier, beginning sustained Germanic incursions into the Swiss plateau.
Roman troops withdraw from the region, leaving it open to settlement by the Alemanni and Burgundians.
The Burgundians settle in western Switzerland, laying the foundations of the area's later French-speaking character.
The Frankish kingdom absorbs the Burgundians, bringing most of the Swiss region under Merovingian control.
Irish missionary Gallus founds the hermitage that grows into the influential Abbey of St. Gallen.
The Treaty of Verdun divides Charlemagne's empire, splitting the Swiss lands between Frankish realms.
The region becomes part of the Holy Roman Empire as the Kingdom of Burgundy passes to Emperor Conrad II.
Duke Berchtold V of Zähringen founds the city of Bern, later a powerful member of the Confederation.
Uri, Schwyz, and Unterwalden swear the Federal Charter of mutual defense, traditionally regarded as the founding of the Swiss Confederation.
Confederate forces crush a Habsburg army at the Battle of Morgarten, securing the young alliance's independence.
Zurich joins the Confederation, strengthening its urban and economic base.
The Confederates defeat and kill Duke Leopold III of Austria at the Battle of Sempach, further weakening Habsburg claims.
Victory in the Swabian War and the Peace of Basel grant the Confederation effective independence from the Holy Roman Empire.
The Confederation reaches thirteen cantons with the admission of Appenzell, completing its early modern core.
Defeat by the French at the Battle of Marignano ends Swiss expansion and steers the Confederation toward lasting neutrality.
Huldrych Zwingli begins preaching in Zurich, launching the Swiss Reformation.
John Calvin establishes a Protestant regime in Geneva, making the city a major center of the Reformation.
The Peace of Westphalia formally recognizes Swiss independence from the Holy Roman Empire.
French revolutionary armies invade and establish the centralized Helvetic Republic, ending the old Confederation.
Napoleon's Act of Mediation restores a federal structure and adds new cantons including Aargau, Vaud, and Ticino.
The Congress of Vienna guarantees Swiss perpetual neutrality and fixes the country's borders.
The brief Sonderbund War defeats a secessionist Catholic alliance and clears the way for a unified federal state.
A new federal constitution transforms the loose confederation into a unified federal state with its capital in Bern.
Henry Dunant and others found the International Committee of the Red Cross in Geneva.
The first Geneva Convention is signed, establishing humanitarian rules of war under Swiss-led diplomacy.
A revised constitution introduces the optional referendum, strengthening Switzerland's tradition of direct democracy.
The Gotthard rail tunnel opens, creating a key Alpine transport link between northern and southern Europe.
The popular initiative is introduced, allowing citizens to propose constitutional amendments by petition.
Switzerland mobilizes its army and maintains armed neutrality throughout the First World War.
Geneva becomes the seat of the newly founded League of Nations, which Switzerland joins while preserving its neutrality.
Switzerland again mobilizes and remains neutral as the Second World War engulfs Europe.
The 'magic formula' establishes a stable multiparty coalition in the Federal Council, a hallmark of Swiss consensus politics.
Switzerland becomes a founding member of the European Free Trade Association (EFTA).
Swiss women gain the right to vote and stand for election at the federal level.
The canton of Jura is created, splitting from Bern after a long autonomy movement.
Voters narrowly reject membership in the European Economic Area, setting Switzerland's path of bilateral deals with the EU.
A fully revised federal constitution comes into force, modernizing the country's basic law.
Switzerland joins the United Nations as a full member following a national referendum.
Switzerland enters the Schengen Area, easing border controls with neighboring European states.
The Gotthard Base Tunnel, the world's longest railway tunnel, opens after nearly two decades of construction.
Voters approve same-sex marriage in a national referendum, granting full marriage rights to same-sex couples.
